questions.stem: What is the index of the first element of a C array?
options: It depends on the array size
questions.explanation: C arrays are zero-indexed, so the first element is at index `0`. For an array of size `n`, the valid indices run from `0` to `n - 1`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Given `int a[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};`, what does `a[2]` equal?
questions.explanation: Indexing starts at `0`, so `a[0]` is `10`, `a[1]` is `20`, and `a[2]` is `30`. The index counts how many steps past the first element you move.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: For an array declared `double v[8];`, what is the largest valid index?
questions.explanation: An array of size `8` has indices `0` through `7`. The index `8` is out of bounds and accessing it is undefined behavior. The valid range is always `0` to `size - 1`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`int a[4] = {2, 4, 6, 8};`
`printf("%d", a[0] + a[3]);`
questions.explanation: `a[0]` is `2` and `a[3]` is `8`, so their sum is `2 + 8 = 10`. The first and last elements of a four-element array are at indices `0` and `3`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this loop print?

`int a[3] = {5, 10, 15};`
`int s = 0;`
`for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) s += a[i];`
`printf("%d", s);`
questions.explanation: The loop adds every element: `5 + 10 + 15 = 30`. This is the standard pattern for summing an array by iterating over its valid indices.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: How are character strings represented in C?
options: As an array of `char` ending with a null terminator `\0`
As a linked list of characters
questions.explanation: C has no native string type. A string is an array of `char` whose end is marked by the null character `\0`. Functions like `printf("%s", ...)` rely on that terminator to know where the string stops.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which character marks the end of a C string?
questions.explanation: Every C string ends with the null terminator `\0`, an invisible character with value `0`. It tells string functions where the text stops. Spaces and newlines are ordinary characters inside a string.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: How many bytes of storage does `char s[] = "cat";` occupy?
questions.explanation: The string `"cat"` holds three letters plus the hidden null terminator `\0`, so the array needs `4` bytes. Forgetting the terminator is a common source of off-by-one bugs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which function from `<string.h>` returns the length of a string, not counting the null terminator?
questions.explanation: `strlen` counts the characters up to but excluding the `\0`. `strcpy` copies a string, `strcat` joins two strings, and `strcmp` compares two strings.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `printf("%d", strlen("hello"));` print?
questions.explanation: `strlen` counts the visible characters only. `"hello"` has five letters, so it returns `5`. The null terminator is not counted, even though it occupies storage.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`char s[] = "DATA";`
`printf("%c", s[1]);`
questions.explanation: A string is an array of characters, so `s[0]` is `D`, `s[1]` is `A`, `s[2]` is `T`, `s[3]` is `A`. Printing `s[1]` with `%c` gives `A`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which specifier prints a whole string with `printf`?
questions.explanation: `%s` prints characters from the given address until it hits the null terminator. `%c` prints just one character, while `%d` and `%f` are for numbers.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.explanation: In a 2D array, the first index selects the row and the second selects the column. Row `1` is `{3, 4}`, and column `0` of that row is `3`. Both indices start at `0`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: How do you declare a 2D array of integers with 3 rows and 4 columns?
questions.explanation: A 2D array uses separate bracket pairs for each dimension: `int m[3][4];`. C does not use a comma inside one bracket for multiple dimensions, so `m[3,4]` is wrong.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`int a[5] = {0};`
`printf("%d", a[3]);`
questions.explanation: Initializing with `{0}` sets the first element to `0` and, because the rest of the initializer is empty, zero-initializes all remaining elements. So `a[3]` is `0`. This is a common way to create a zeroed array.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print after modifying an element?

`int a[4] = {1, 2, 3, 4};`
`a[2] = 99;`
`printf("%d", a[2]);`
questions.explanation: The assignment `a[2] = 99;` overwrites the element at index `2`, which was `3`, with `99`. Reading `a[2]` afterward gives the new value `99`. Array elements are mutable through their index.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Which `<string.h>` function copies the contents of one string into another?
questions.explanation: `strcpy(dest, src)` copies the characters of `src`, including its `\0`, into `dest`. `strlen` measures length, `strcmp` compares, and `strchr` searches for a character.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What does `strcmp("abc", "abc")` return?
questions.explanation: `strcmp` returns `0` when the two strings are equal. It returns a negative or positive value when they differ, depending on which string is lexicographically smaller or larger. Equal strings give exactly `0`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this loop print?

`char s[] = "abc";`
`for (int i = 0; s[i] != '\0'; i++) printf("%c", s[i]);`
questions.explanation: The loop walks through the characters and stops when it reaches the null terminator `\0`, which is the standard idiom for traversing a C string. It prints `a`, `b`, `c` and then halts, so the output is `abc`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: What happens when you read or write an array element outside its valid index range in C?
options: The index automatically wraps around to a valid one
The compiler refuses to build the program
It is undefined behavior; C does not check array bounds
questions.explanation: C performs no bounds checking, so accessing an out-of-range index is undefined behavior. It may crash, corrupt memory, or appear to work by accident. The programmer is responsible for staying within `0` to `size - 1`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: How many elements does the array `int a[] = {3, 6, 9, 12, 15};` have?
questions.explanation: When the size is omitted and an initializer list is given, the array size is the number of listed values. There are five values, so the array has `5` elements with indices `0` to `4`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print (largest element search)?

`int a[4] = {3, 9, 2, 7};`
`int max = a[0];`
`for (int i = 1; i < 4; i++) if (a[i] > max) max = a[i];`
`printf("%d", max);`
questions.explanation: The loop keeps the largest value seen so far. Starting with `3`, it updates `max` to `9` (since `9 > 3`), and `9` stays the maximum because no later element exceeds it. The output is `9`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem:
What does this code print?

`char c = 'Z';`
`char arr[2] = {c, '\0'};`
`printf("%s", arr);`
questions.explanation: The array holds the character `Z` followed by the null terminator, forming a valid one-character string. Printing it with `%s` outputs `Z` and stops at the `\0`. The terminator itself is not displayed.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Work with array indexing and bounds.
questions.solution: For `int a[6] = {4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24};`, the value `a[3]` equals {{1}}. The largest index you may legally access is {{2}}. The sum `a[0] + a[5]` equals {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Counting from index `0`, the element `a[3]` is the fourth value, `16`. A size-`6` array has indices `0` to `5`, so `5` is the largest valid index. The first and last values are `4` and `24`, summing to `28`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Reason about C strings and their storage.
questions.solution: A C string is stored as an array of `char` ending in the special terminator character {{1}}. The function that returns the number of visible characters is {{2}}. For the literal `"hello"`, that function returns {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Strings end with the null terminator `\0`. `strlen` reports the count of characters before that terminator, and `"hello"` has five letters, so it returns `5`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Predict string storage size and a comparison result.
questions.solution: The declaration `char w[] = "grid";` reserves {{1}} bytes, because the four letters need one extra byte for the terminator. The call `strcmp("sun", "sun")` returns {{2}}, while the format specifier used to print an entire string with `printf` is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: `"grid"` is four letters plus a `\0`, so the array is `5` bytes. `strcmp` returns `0` for equal strings. An entire string prints with `%s`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Index into a two-dimensional array.
questions.solution: For `int m[2][3] = {{1, 2, 3}, {4, 5, 6}};`, the element `m[1][2]` equals {{1}}. The first index selects the {{2}} and the second selects the column. The element `m[0][0]` equals {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Row `1` is `{4, 5, 6}` and column `2` of that row is `6`. By convention the first subscript is the row index and the second is the column. The top-left element `m[0][0]` is `1`.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Trace an array sum and average.
questions.solution: Given `int a[4] = {10, 20, 30, 40};`, a loop summing all elements produces {{1}}. Dividing that integer sum by `4` using integer division gives {{2}}. The number of elements (the loop count) is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The elements add to `10 + 20 + 30 + 40 = 100`. Integer division `100 / 4` is exactly `25`. The array has four elements, so the summing loop runs four times.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Pick the right string functions for common tasks.
questions.solution: To join the text of one string onto the end of another, use {{1}}. To check whether two strings hold identical text, use {{2}}, expecting a return value of {{3}} when they match.
questions.explanation: `strcat` appends one string to another. `strcmp` compares two strings and returns `0` precisely when they are equal.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)

questions.stem: Reason about array bounds safety.
questions.solution: For `double x[10];`, valid indices run from `0` up to {{1}}. Accessing `x[10]` is {{2}} because C performs no bounds checking. The total number of elements in the array is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: A size-`10` array uses indices `0` to `9`, so `9` is the largest valid one. Reading `x[10]` is out of bounds and therefore undefined behavior. The array holds ten elements in all.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Scientific Computing course (C and MPI)
